revision.text: {{Draft}}'''{{PAGENAME}}''' is a [[Beppyo shrine]] {{Draft Categories|[[Category:Beppyo shrines]]}}

{{Draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''{{ill|Kōzu-gū|ja|高津宮|simple}}''' is a shrine in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors Emperor Nintoku. He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku saw little smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, Emperor Seiwa became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" />
During Emperor Ōgimachi's reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. Hideyoshi was building Ōsaka Castle then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, Ōsaka City's official song was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for rakugo. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. Katsura Bunshi V, a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has an annual festival. This celebrates its history and culture.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:高津宮]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Draft Categories|[[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] }}

In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, Ōsaka City's official song was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for rakugo. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. Katsura Bunshi V, a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has an annual festival. This celebrates its history and culture.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:高津宮]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Draft Categories|[[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] }}

It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last=Berna |first=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA41&dq=Kozu-gu&hl=en |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku saw little smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />

During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI|Katsura Bunshi V,]] a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has an annual festival. This celebrates its history and culture.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:高津宮]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Draft Categories|[[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to empress Jingū]] }} {{Hachiman Faith}}

{{Draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''{{ill|Kōzu-gū|ja|高津宮|simple}}''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last=Berna |first=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA41&dq=Kozu-gu&hl=en |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku saw little smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI|Katsura Bunshi VI,]] a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has an annual festival. This celebrates its history and culture.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:高津宮]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Draft Categories|[[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to empress Jingū]] [[Category:Rakugo]] }} {{Hachiman Faith}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last=Berna |first=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA41&dq=Kozu-gu&hl=en |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI|Katsura Bunshi VI,]] a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />

It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref> [[ja:高津宮]] ==References== {{reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last=Berna |first=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA41&dq=Kozu-gu&hl=en |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref> [[ja:高津宮]] ==References== {{reflist}}

The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

{{DEFAULTSORT:Kozu-gu}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[en:Kōzu-gū]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to empress Jingū]] [[Category:Rakugo]] [[en:Kōzu-gū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[en:Kōzu-gū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia01]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia18]] [[en:Kōzu-gū]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />

The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>

In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />

The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 高津宮.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Nintoku]] , [[Emperor Ojin]] , [[Emperor Chuai]] , [[Empress Jingu]] , [[Emperor Richu]] , Empress Ashihime (葦姫皇后) }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>
