revision.comment: Bot: remove incorrect Shona (sn:) interwiki links
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11671792]] Wikidata name: Kōzu-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:高津宮.jpg | deity = {{ill|Nintoku|qid=Q313119}} | location = {{ill|Q60996268|qid=Q60996268}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.66886111|N|135.51386111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[:en:Emperor Seiwa|Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[:en:Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[:en:Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka City Anthem|qq=|1=Osaka City Anthem|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18383267}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[:en:Katsura Bunshi VI|Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[:en:Emperor Seiwa|Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[:en:Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[:en:Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka City Anthem|qq=|1=Osaka City Anthem|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18383267}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[:en:Katsura Bunshi VI|Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

[[en:Kōzu-gū]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Kozu-Gu}} [[ja:高津宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11671792}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kozu-Gu]][[en:Kōzu-gū]][[ja:高津宮]][[pl:Kōzu-gū]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11671792]] Wikidata name: Kōzu-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:高津宮.jpg | deity = {{ill|Nintoku|WD=Q313119}} | location = {{ill|Q60996268|WD=Q60996268}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.66886111|N|135.51386111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[:en:Emperor Seiwa|Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[:en:Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[:en:Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka City Anthem|1=Osaka City Anthem|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18383267}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[:en:Katsura Bunshi VI|Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kozu-Gu]][[en:Kōzu-gū]][[ja:高津宮]][[pl:Kōzu-gū]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11671792]] Wikidata name: Kōzu-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:高津宮.jpg | deity = {{ill|Nintoku|WD=Q313119}} | location = {{ill|Q60996268|WD=Q60996268}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.66886111|N|135.51386111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[:en:Emperor Seiwa|Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[:en:Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[:en:Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka City Anthem|1=Osaka City Anthem|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18383267}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[:en:Katsura Bunshi VI|Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, [[:en:Emperor Seiwa|Emperor Seiwa]] became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During [[:en:Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi's]] reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was building [[:en:Osaka Castle|Ōsaka Castle]] then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka City Anthem|1=Osaka City Anthem|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18383267}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. [[:en:Katsura Bunshi VI|Katsura Bunshi VI]], a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kozu-Gu]][[en:Kōzu-gū]][[ja:高津宮]][[pl:Kōzu-gū]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kozu-Gu]][[en:Kōzu-gū]][[ja:高津宮]][[pl:Kōzu-gū]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kozu-Gu]][[en:Kōzu-gū]][[ja:高津宮]][[pl:Kōzu-gū]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11671792]] Wikidata name: Kōzu-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:高津宮.jpg | deity = {{ill|Nintoku|WD=Q313119}} | location = {{ill|Q60996268|WD=Q60996268}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.66886111|N|135.51386111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku}}.<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, {{ill|Emperor Seiwa|en|Emperor Seiwa|WD=Q349243|lt=Emperor Seiwa}} became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During {{ill|Emperor Ōgimachi|en|Emperor Ōgimachi|WD=Q334076|lt=Emperor Ōgimachi's}} reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} was building {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Ōsaka Castle}} then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka City Anthem|1=Osaka City Anthem|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18383267}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. {{ill|Katsura Bunshi VI|en|Katsura Bunshi VI|WD=Q1281916|lt=Katsura Bunshi VI}}, a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kozu-Gu]][[en:Kōzu-gū]][[ja:高津宮]][[pl:Kōzu-gū]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kozu-Gu]][[en:Kōzu-gū]][[ja:高津宮]][[pl:Kōzu-gū]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kozu-Gu]][[en:Kōzu-gū]][[ja:高津宮]][[pl:Kōzu-gū]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11671792]] Wikidata name: Kōzu-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:高津宮.jpg | deity = {{ill|Nintoku|WD=Q313119}} | location = {{ill|Q60996268|WD=Q60996268}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.66886111|N|135.51386111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku}}.<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, {{ill|Emperor Seiwa|en|Emperor Seiwa|WD=Q349243|lt=Emperor Seiwa}} became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During {{ill|Emperor Ōgimachi|en|Emperor Ōgimachi|WD=Q334076|lt=Emperor Ōgimachi's}} reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} was building {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Ōsaka Castle}} then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka City Anthem|1=Osaka City Anthem|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18383267}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. {{ill|Katsura Bunshi VI|en|Katsura Bunshi VI|WD=Q1281916|lt=Katsura Bunshi VI}}, a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka City Anthem|1=Osaka City Anthem|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18383267}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. {{ill|Katsura Bunshi VI|en|Katsura Bunshi VI|WD=Q1281916|lt=Katsura Bunshi VI}}, a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kozu-Gu]][[en:Kōzu-gū]][[ja:高津宮]][[pl:Kōzu-gū]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kozu-Gu]][[en:Kōzu-gū]][[ja:高津宮]][[pl:Kōzu-gū]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kozu-Gu]][[en:Kōzu-gū]][[ja:高津宮]][[pl:Kōzu-gū]]
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:高津宮.jpg | deity = {{ill|Nintoku|WD=Q313119}} | location = {{ill|Q60996268|WD=Q60996268}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.66886111|N|135.51386111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku}}.<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, {{ill|Emperor Seiwa|en|Emperor Seiwa|WD=Q349243|lt=Emperor Seiwa}} became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During {{ill|Emperor Ōgimachi|en|Emperor Ōgimachi|WD=Q334076|lt=Emperor Ōgimachi's}} reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} was building {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Ōsaka Castle}} then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka City Anthem|1=Osaka City Anthem|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18383267}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. {{ill|Katsura Bunshi VI|en|Katsura Bunshi VI|WD=Q1281916|lt=Katsura Bunshi VI}}, a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

During {{ill|Emperor Ōgimachi|en|Emperor Ōgimachi|WD=Q334076|lt=Emperor Ōgimachi's}} reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} was building {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Ōsaka Castle}} then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka City Anthem|1=Osaka City Anthem|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18383267}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. {{ill|Katsura Bunshi VI|en|Katsura Bunshi VI|WD=Q1281916|lt=Katsura Bunshi VI}}, a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

It honors {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}}.<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, {{ill|Emperor Seiwa|en|Emperor Seiwa|WD=Q349243|lt=Emperor Seiwa|1=Emperor Seiwa}} became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During {{ill|Emperor Ōgimachi|en|Emperor Ōgimachi|WD=Q334076|lt=Emperor Ōgimachi's|1=Emperor Ōgimachi}} reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} was building {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Ōsaka Castle|1=Osaka Castle}} then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka City Anthem|1=Osaka City Anthem|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18383267}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. {{ill|Katsura Bunshi VI|en|Katsura Bunshi VI|WD=Q1281916|lt=Katsura Bunshi VI|1=Katsura Bunshi VI}}, a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka}}
{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:高津宮.jpg | deity = {{ill|Nintoku|qid=Q313119|1=Emperor Nintoku}} | location = {{ill|Q60996268|qid=Q60996268|1=Kōzu}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.66886111|N|135.51386111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kōzu-gū''' is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in Ōsaka, Japan. It honors {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|qid=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}}.<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Berna |first1=Cristina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XFHTEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kozu-gu&pg=PA41 |title=Yoshitaki Kunikazu Nansuitei Yoshiyuki 100 Views of Osaka |last2=Thomsen |first2=Eric |date=2023-08-28 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-7578-0772-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-13 |title=Kozugu Shrine（高津宮） {{!}} Maido |url=https://maido-bob.osaka/en/spot/kozugu-shrine/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=maido-bob.osaka |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=author |date=2016-11-18 |title=Kouzu Gu Shrine - 大阪中心 The Heart of Osaka Japan- 大阪市 中央区 オフィシャルサイト 観光・イベント総合情報ポータルサイト |url=https://osaka-chushin.jp/en/spot/3886 |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en-US}}</ref> He was the 16th emperor. He reigned from 313 to 399. Emperor Nintoku named the area Kōzu-gū. This area is now known as Ōsaka.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kōzu-gū 高津宮 {{!}} Ōsaka-shi, Chūō-ku {{!}} Rods Shinto |url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/27-114-04 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=shintoshrines |language=en}}</ref>
One day, Emperor Nintoku looked at the city and he didn't see much smoke from cooking in the city. This meant the people were poor. He removed all taxes to help them. This made him very popular.<ref name=":0" />
In 866, {{ill|Emperor Seiwa|en|Emperor Seiwa|qid=Q349243|lt=Emperor Seiwa|1=Emperor Seiwa}} became interested in this place. He was the 56th emperor. He reigned from 858 to 876. He ordered a search for the old capital's ruins. A shrine was built on the site. This became the Kōzu-gū shrine.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
During {{ill|Emperor Ōgimachi|en|Emperor Ōgimachi|qid=Q334076|lt=Emperor Ōgimachi's|1=Emperor Ōgimachi}} reign, changes happened. He reigned from 1577 to 1586. {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} was building {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|qid=Q321242|lt=Ōsaka Castle|1=Osaka Castle}} then. The shrine was in the castle area. In 1583, the shrine's shintai was moved. It went to Himekoso Jinja. This is Kōzu-gū's current location.<ref name=":0" />
In 1872, Kōzu-gū became a Prefectural Shrine. In 1921, the {{ill|Osaka City Anthem|ja|大阪市歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka City Anthem|1=Osaka City Anthem|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18383267}} was made. It mentions Emperor Nintoku. It talks about the city's prosperity.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine was almost destroyed in 1945. This was during US air raids. It was rebuilt by 1961.<ref name=":0" />
Kōzu-gū is important for [[rakugo]]. Rakugo is a Japanese art form. Many rakugo shows have been held here. {{ill|Katsura Bunshi VI|en|Katsura Bunshi VI|qid=Q1281916|lt=Katsura Bunshi VI|1=Katsura Bunshi VI}}, a famous artist, performed here in 2015.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine has several deities. The main deity is Emperor Nintoku. There are subordinate shrines. These include Himekoso Jinja, Takakura Inari, and Yaui Inari Jinja. Tani Massha has more shrines. These are Shiragiku Jinja, Shinnen Jinja, and Jōkō Jinja.<ref name=":0" />
It holds a cherry blossom festival on April 7th.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-04-04 |title=Cherry blossom festival at Kouzu-gu｜MITSUWAYA |url=https://mitsuwaya.tesen.jp/en/journal/kouzugu_cherryblossom |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=MITSUWAYA｜Official Website |language=ja}}</ref>
